{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.7, "R1.2": 0.9, "R1.3": 1.0, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.6": 0.5, "R1.7": 0.8}}

Critique of the section. Several substantive problems, ordered by severity.

**1. The novelty/contribution claim contradicts itself.** The text says SentiBridge is the "first" cross-lingual sentiment classifier with competitive performance across high- and low-resource languages, but then grounds the claim on a "novel training procedure ... as introduced by Smith et al. (2022)." If the procedure was introduced by prior work, it is not novel here, and it cannot underwrite a "first" claim. Either (a) the contribution is the application/extension to sentiment with the new pooling layer and joint multilingual head — in which case drop "novel training procedure" and reframe — or (b) there is genuine methodological novelty that needs to be described independently of Smith et al. Pick one and rewrite.

Relatedly, the "first cross-lingual sentiment classifier ... across high- and low-resource languages" claim is almost certainly too strong. Cross-lingual sentiment work using mBERT, XLM-R, and similar backbones has covered exactly this territory for years. This needs softening or a precise scoping qualifier (e.g., "first to combine X and Y for sentiment").

**2. Evidence does not support the headline claim.** The contribution claim is "competitive across both high- and low-resource languages," but:
- The six training languages listed (English, Spanish, French, German, Mandarin, Arabic) are all high- or mid-resource. No low-resource language appears.
- Evaluation is on XNLI-sentiment (5 languages, presumably overlapping with training) and SST (English, in-distribution). There is no held-out low-resource evaluation.
- Only two per-language numbers are reported in prose (Spanish, Mandarin), with the rest deferred to "Table 4." For a results-bearing paragraph, at minimum report aggregate numbers and identify the weakest language.
- Baselines: only a "translation-pipeline baseline" is named. No comparison to obvious direct-transfer baselines (mBERT, XLM-R fine-tuned on English sentiment, etc.), which is the standard bar in this subfield.

The gap between claim and evidence is the most serious issue. Either restrict the claim to the languages actually evaluated, or add low-resource evaluation.

**3. Internal logical issue with the Liu et al. framing.** The paragraph contrasts "existing methods" (translation pipelines, single high-resource focus) with the proposed direct approach, then immediately attributes the direct approach to Liu et al. (2019). This collapses the contrast: if Liu et al. already did direct cross-lingual sentiment on Romance/Germanic languages, then "existing methods either focus on ... or use translation pipelines" is false as written, and the delta over Liu et al. needs to be stated explicitly (more languages? non-Indo-European coverage? architectural difference?).

**4. Methodological underspecification.** "1.2M sentiment-labeled examples per language drawn from public corpora" — which corpora? Label schema alignment across languages (binary? 3-way? 5-way?) is unspecified and matters for the XNLI-sentiment numbers. "Attention-based pooling layer" is too vague to reproduce or assess. "Multilingual contrastive pretraining" — on what pairs, with what objective?

**5. Citations.** Smith et al. (2022) and Liu et al. (2019) are placeholders without titles/venues here; verify they exist and are correctly attributed before submission. Missing citations for the obvious reference points: XLM-R (Conneau et al.), mBERT, and prior cross-lingual sentiment work (e.g., Barnes, Mohammad, Chen et al.). The current citation set is too thin to support either the novelty claim or the framing of "existing methods."

**6. Structural suggestion.** The section currently mixes contribution claim, related work, method, and results in a compressed way. For a paper section this would read better as: (i) problem and gap, (ii) what we propose (with crisp delta vs Smith et al. and Liu et al.), (iii) training setup, (iv) evaluation setup and baselines, (v) headline results, (vi) scoped contribution statement. The closing "meaningful contribution" sentence is filler and should be replaced by a specific takeaway (e.g., the size of the
